A non-LLM program owns content, structure, consistency, difficulty, and ground truth, using the world-state and construction-solving asymmetry; the LLM only renders a given local fragment fluently. Round-trip verification parses rendered text back to structure and discards mismatches.

The generation pipeline is restructured so the LLM stops making content decisions. A program samples the world-state and a task within it, decides what must appear in a document, and knows the correct answer by construction. The LLM receives only a local fragment and a rendering instruction, and produces fluent text. The program then parses the LLM's output back into structure and compares it to what was specified; mismatches are discarded rather than corrected, bounding rendering error without needing the LLM to be reliable.

This is the proposed resolution to LLM-as-author diversity contraction (node 35), built on the persistent world-state (node 36) for consistency and the construction-solving asymmetry (node 37) for generating problems harder than the generator LLM could solve itself. Round-trip verification is the check that closes the loop: since the program knows the intended structure, it can attempt to recover that structure from the rendered text by parsing, not by re-asking an LLM to judge, and drop any document where the rendering failed to preserve it, rather than trusting the LLM's fluency as a proxy for correctness. This is a substantial rework of the generate stage (node 11), not yet implemented; it would apply first to the deferred reasoning-era text types (node 22, node 23) and to the in-context binding validation tasks (node 33).
